steps.ordered: CT Abdomen (CT ABD & PELVIS WITH CONTRAST)

ensemble.other_hypothesis: adhesion-related pathology / internal hernia, pelvic/gynecologic source (though post-hysterectomy), colitis (infectious or ischemic), abscess, or other colonic process
ensemble.information_gap: The anatomic source and nature of the LLQ/suprapubic inflammatory process is unknown — no imaging has been performed yet, so the physician cannot distinguish diverticulitis (with or without complication such as abscess or perforation) from colitis, adhesion-related pathology, or another intra-abdominal process causing the fever and leukocytosis.
ensemble.expected_finding: Pericolonic fat stranding with colonic wall thickening in the sigmoid/descending colon consistent with diverticulitis, possibly with a pericolic abscess or free air; alternatively, evidence of colitis, bowel obstruction from adhesions, or another focal inflammatory/infectious process in the LLQ or pelvis.
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: The combination of LLQ/suprapubic tenderness, fever (T 99.1), leukocytosis (WBC 17.6), nearly one week of worsening pain, and no prior imaging makes CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast the standard first-line imaging study to characterize the source, extent, and any complications of the intra-abdominal process.
grounding: Physical examination: 'focally tender in LLQ, suprapubic region'
History: 'nearly 1 week of abdominal pain with fevers... intermittent fevers and chills... pain worsened today'
History: 'last colonoscopy... difficult given the angulation of her sigmoid'
History: 'PSH: dx laparoscopy/LOA, hysterectomy, cholecystectomy, appendectomy'
Imaging already resulted BEFORE this decision point: '(none)'
ensemble.reasoning: This patient presents with nearly one week of progressive LLQ and suprapubic pain, low-grade fever, and a markedly elevated WBC with neutrophilia — a classic inflammatory picture without prior imaging to guide me. Her history of a tortuous sigmoid colon on prior colonoscopy raises diverticulitis as the leading concern, but her extensive prior abdominal surgery (LOA, hysterectomy, appendectomy) keeps adhesion-related pathology and other colonic processes on the table. CT abdomen and pelvis with IV contrast is the most efficient single test to identify the anatomic source, characterize any complications (abscess, perforation, obstruction), and guide disposition and management.

vindication.actual_finding: CT demonstrated sigmoid colonic wall thickening, hyperemia, pericolonic fat stranding, and adjacent pockets of local free air in the region of diverticula, consistent with acute complicated sigmoid diverticulitis with microperforation, without a drainable abscess or large free air.
vindication.relation_to_expectation: The actual finding directly matched the physician's primary expected finding of pericolonic fat stranding with colonic wall thickening in the sigmoid colon consistent with diverticulitis, and additionally confirmed a complication (microperforation) as the physician had anticipated as a possibility.
